note: SYNTHETIC SAMPLE DATA. These are placeholder posts written to demonstrate the app, NOT real tweets by @aleabitoreddit. They are replaced automatically the first time the scraper runs successfully.

tweets.text: AI capex keeps ramping but the market still prices the obvious layer. The scarce layer is power delivery, not compute. $VRT backlog tells the story — thermal + power is sold out through next year.
Everyone screens GPUs. Almost nobody screens who makes the 800G optical links between them. $COHR $LITE $FN — the interconnect layer is tight and qualification cycles are long.
HBM is the classic bottleneck setup: three suppliers, brutal yield curve, capacity reserved years out. $MU still looks mispriced relative to how constrained the layer is.

Networking is where the AI story gets underwritten. $ANET sits between hyperscaler demand and merchant silicon — strong position, but watch white-box risk. What would prove me wrong: a major cloud moving to fully self-built switching.
Retimers and active electrical cables are a quiet chokepoint as rack density explodes. $CRDO $ALAB — small layer, few credible suppliers, long customer certification.

Liquid cooling is transitioning from optional to required. $VRT and $MOD both benefit, but the evidence quality differs a lot — one has contracted backlog, the other has a story. Rank the layer first, then the company.
Grid connection is the hardest constraint in the whole AI chain. Switchgear lead times are 60+ weeks. $POWL is a boring company sitting on a genuinely scarce layer.
The market treats EMS as commodity. But rack-scale AI integration is not commodity assembly — $CLS keeps winning programs because the know-how is real. Margin trajectory is the tell.
Advanced packaging metrology is tiny but critical. Every HBM stack needs inspection. $ONTO $CAMT — when yield is the constraint, the tools that find defects get pricing power.
Custom silicon vs merchant GPU debate misses the point — both need the same scarce layers underneath: CoWoS, HBM, optics, power. $AVGO benefits either way. That's the position you want.

Glass substrates are the next packaging transition to watch. $GLW has decades of glass know-how the market files under 'TV screens'. Early, unverified lead — needs capacity evidence before it's a thesis.

Server ODM margins tell you who has leverage in the chain. $SMCI moves boxes but the scarce layers sit upstream. Volume growth is not the same thing as a moat — be careful with the crowded story here.
Checked the switchgear channel again: lead times still extending. $POWL backlog up, and the bid pipeline includes multiple gigawatt-scale data center projects. The grid layer stays tight into 2027.
Optical module pricing held up better than feared this quarter. $COHR indium phosphide capacity is the layer to watch — datacom lasers are supply constrained, not demand constrained.

Reminder on process: market story → system change → required parts → chain layers → scarce constraint → companies → evidence → what could prove it wrong. Skip a step and you own a narrative, not a thesis.
$MU earnings confirmed the setup: HBM sold out for the calendar year, bit share gains, pricing up. The layer is doing the work. Main risk is DRAM cycle rolling over faster than HBM mix shifts.

Power transformers might be an even tighter layer than switchgear. Long lead times, few qualified suppliers, no fast capacity response. Still building the candidate list — $POWL adjacent but not the pure play.
1.6T optics qualification is starting. The vendors that pass first lock the sockets for years. $FN builds for everyone and doesn't have to pick the winning standard — underrated position.
Cooling distribution units are becoming a real market. $VRT guided above consensus again — thermal is compounding, not a one-off. Watching whether competitors can close the service-network gap.
$ANET quietly expanding into AI-tuned routing. The market debate is stuck on switch share while the attach story grows. Evidence: two hyperscaler design wins disclosed in the last call.
Be skeptical of any AI infra name that can't answer 'what exactly do you constrain?' in one sentence. $CRDO can: high-speed copper reach at low power. That's why it stays on the priority list.
Weekly digest: layers I'd research first right now — (1) grid + power equipment, (2) HBM/memory interconnect, (3) 800G→1.6T optics, (4) liquid cooling. Compute itself ranks below all four.
$ALAB expanding beyond retimers into full connectivity platforms. Customer concentration is the real risk — two customers are most of revenue. Watch the 10-Q disclosure, not the keynote.
The EMS debate on $CLS comes down to one question: is rack-scale integration know-how durable or commoditizable? Margin expansion three quarters running says durable, for now.
